citation.title: Solvent stable thin-film composite membranes with a COF interlayer for artemisinin enrichment

content: A B S T R A C T Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) is an energy-efficient method for pharmaceutical separation that preserves bioactivity while enabling solvent recycling. Polyamide (PA) thin-film composite (TFC) membranes have demonstrated great potential for this application because of their easy fabrication, robust stability in solvents, and excellent separation performance. However, achieving controllable interfacial polymerization (IP) of the PA layer on a solvent-resistant support remains a challenge. To address this issue, a sulfonated COF–Na+ interlayer was developed and deposited onto nylon, providing a smooth and uniform surface that facilitates the formation of a continuous and defect-free PA layer. The resulting membrane exhibited a high methanol permeance of 9.95 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 with excellent rejection above 94% for both charged and uncharged molecules. This approach was successfully applied to both organic and inorganic microfiltration substrates, demonstrating that this COF interlayer effectively eliminates variations in support materials by providing a uniform and smooth surface for the IP process. In this work, the resulting membrane demonstrated over 98% artemisinin rejection in ethanol, making it highly efficient for concentrating artemisinin while simultaneously recycling ethanol, thereby reducing energy consumption and minimizing solvent waste.

citation.title: Solvent stable thin-film composite membranes with a COF interlayer for artemisinin enrichment

content: 1. Introduction Organic solvents play a dominant role in the pharmaceutical industry because of their ability to dissolve a wide range of compounds, facili­tating essential processes such as drug synthesis, purification, and extraction. Many active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) exhibit limited solubility in water, resulting in organic solvents accounting for 80–90% of utilization chemicals in the pharmaceutical industry [1]. The extensive use of organic solvents has caused major concerns regarding environmental pollution and human health risks. Moreover, the recov­ery of high-value APIs from organic solvents is indispensable in phar­maceutical production and involves several energy-intensive and costly processes, such as crystallization or chromatography [2]. To develop more energy-efficient and cost-efficient alternatives to concentrate APIs while minimizing solvent waste, organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) has emerged as a crucial technology for enriching pharmaceutical compounds such as antibiotics, peptides or pharmaceutical in­termediates while recycling solvents [3]. Unlike crystallization, which requires high temperatures, OSN operates under mild conditions, pre­venting the thermal degradation of the APIs [4]. Compared with chro­matography, which consumes a large amount of solvent, OSN enables efficient separation with significantly lower solvent consumption, making it a more environmentally sustainable and cost-effective option for pharmaceutical separation.

Artemisinin is a well-known natural antimalarial drug. The discovery of artemisinin was awarded the 2015 Nobel Prize in Medicine or Phys­iology to the Chinese scientist Youyou Tu, who first identified its effective treatment for malaria [5]. The active component of artemisinin is generally extracted from a Chinese medical plant, Artemisia annua using organic solvents at elevated temperatures and reflux (e.g. at 40 ◦C) [6]. The commonly used solvents include ethanol, n-hexane, toluene and petroleum ether [6]. Since artemisinin is highly thermally unstable [7], OSN technology would be an ideal process for extracting artemisinin by concentrating the artemisinin while simultaneously recycling the sol­vent without destroying its pharmaceutical efficacy. Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) processes require membranes

Thinfilm composite (TFC) membranes—comprising a selective separation layer supported by a solvent-stable interlayer or substrate—are prom­ising candidates for OSN because their layered architecture enables in­dependent optimization of each layer's physicochemical properties [3]. Polyamide (PA)-based TFC membranes are widely used in aqueous separations due to their straightforward fabrication and excellent per­formance. These membranes typically consist of three layers: a PA top layer that provides solute rejection, an interlayer that governs interfacial properties during PA formation, and a bottom substrate that supplies mechanical strength. Selecting an appropriate interlayer is crucial, as it must support the uniform growth of a defect-free PA film. Common in­terlayers include ultrafiltration membranes made from polyimide (PI), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polysulfone (PSF), and polyethersulfone (PES). However, many of these polymers lack stability in organic solvents. To improve their suitability for OSN, additional post-treatments such as cross-linking or wet/dry annealing are often required, adding complexity to the fabrication process and sometimes limiting scalability beyond the laboratory [3].

content: ng or wet/dry annealing are often required, adding complexity to the fabrication process and sometimes limiting scalability beyond the laboratory [3]. Moreover, such post treatments may induce pore collapse and structural aging, consequently deteriorating the membrane's permeance and mechanical integrity [8]. In contrast, many inorganic interlayers are extremely stable in harsh solvents but suffer from poor compatibility with the PA selective layer, which results in defects and thereby compromises membrane performance [9]. To address these challenges, commercial microfiltration (MF) membranes with inherent solvent stability have been employed as substrates for directly preparing PA TFC membranes without an inter­layer. Kosaraju et al. successfully fabricated PA nanofilms on a micro­porous polypropylene (PP) support, which demonstrated excellent solvent resistance to alcohols and aromatic hydrocarbons. However, the large surface pore size (117 nm * 42 nm) and intrinsic hydrophobicity of PP present challenges in synthesizing a dense active layer, which leads to relatively low solute rejections—88% for brilliant blue R and 43% for safranin O in methanol [10]. Li et al. fabricated a PA rejection layer on a nylon 66 substrate directly, achieving high stability in dimethylforma­mide (DMF) and high rejection of acid fuchsin in ethanol (98%). How­ever, the excessive use of monomers, including MPD and TMC, makes the process less sustainable and economical [11].

Therefore, these studies demonstrated difficulties of achieving a high-quality, defect-free PA layer via an IP reaction when solvent-resistant MF substrates are directly used [12]. In solvent stable membranes, researchers have explored incorporating interlayers which have appropriate interfacial properties for the formation of high-quality PA separation layers [13]. For example, in the research of Zhou et al., two layers of smaller alpha-alumina particles followed by carbon nanotubes were used to smooth the surface of a commercial α-Al2O3 flat sheet support with a pore size of 2 μm, which allowed for the growth of a PA layer with good separation performance [8]. Lu et al. also coated a layer of single-walled carbon nanotubes with polydopamine (PD/SWCNT) on a PTFE substrate to reduce the average pore size from 465 nm to 55 nm for the fabrication of PA films [14]. These examples demonstrated that depositing an interlayer on commercial MF substrates is an effective strategy to reduce the surface pore size and roughness without causing pore collapse or compromising the mechanical strength, thereby enabling the fabrication of PA TFC membranes.

In recent years, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have gained significant attention as promising interlayer materials because of their well-defined porous structure, high chemical stability, and tunable functionality. Zhang [15] used a TpBd-(SO3)2 COF interlayer to increase the storage of monomers and reduce their diffusion rate, leading to a thinner layer of PA on PES supports. This work mainly focused on the structural and hydrophilic advantages of sulfonated COF interlayers in regulating PA growth, whereas the potential influence of their acidic nature on IP has not been explicitly discussed. Yang [16] constructed an interlayer of TpPa COF to improve the hydrophilicity of the PI supports to manipulate the IP process. These works demonstrated the regulatory effect of the COF interlayer on PA formation atop ultrafiltration sub­strates. However, the effect of the COF interlayer on MF substrates has rarely been explored and warrants further investigation. This study developed a sulfonated COF interlayer for PA TFC mem­brane fabrication on commercial MF substrates, and the fabrication process is presented in Fig. 1.

content: eveloped a sulfonated COF interlayer for PA TFC mem­brane fabrication on commercial MF substrates, and the fabrication process is presented in Fig. 1. Specifically, a state-of-the-art single-phase synthesis method was employed to produce a high-yield dispersion of 2D COF nanosheets using 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid (DABA) and 1,3,5-trifor­mylphloroglucinol (TFP) as monomers. The sulfonated COF nanosheets were then coated onto a MF nylon 66 substrate via vacuum-assisted selfassembly under the aggregation effect of methanol, forming a smooth and uniform COF–H+ interlayer with a reduced pore size. To further optimize the membrane performance, post treatment of the COF–H+ interlayer was conducted with NaHCO3 or NaOH, resulting in a COF–Na+ interlayer. This modification fixed the negative effects of H+ on the IP process, enabling the formation of a continuous and defect-free PA layer. The resulting PA–COF–Na+-nylon TFC membrane exhibited outstanding separation performance for both charged and uncharged dye molecules, achieving rejections exceeding 94% across different dyes. Furthermore, the versatility of the COF–Na+ interlayer was demonstrated by successfully applying the approach to inorganic MF substrates, such as yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) hollow fibres, which achieved comparable rejection performance. The optimized PA–COF–Na+-nylon membrane achieved over 98% rejection of artemi­sinin from ethanol, facilitating its enrichment while simultaneously enabling solvent recovery.

This innovation not only enhances the effi­ciency of artemisinin extraction but also significantly reduces energy consumption and solvent waste, making it an environmentally friendly and economically sustainable solution. Overall, this study developed a simple and highly effective strategy for fabricating high-performance PA TFC membranes through COFbased interlayer engineering. The method demonstrates remarkable versatility and reproducibility, offering promising potential for broader applications in membrane-based pharmaceutical separation. 2. Experimental 2.1. The suspension composition and spinning conditions are provided in Table S1. 2.2.2. Synthesis of DABA-TFP COF nanosheets The DABA-TFP COF nanosheets were synthesized via the single so­lution‒phase methodology described by Huang et al. [18]. The mono­mers DABA (172 mg) and TFP (128 mg) were separately dissolved in 50 mL of DMSO. The TFP/DMSO solution was added dropwise to the DABA/DMSO solution under gentle stirring. The resulting 100 mL mixture was then left undisturbed at room temperature for 48 h to facilitate the polymerization process. The colloidal suspension of DABA-TFP COF nanosheets was subsequently dialyzed against DI water for 72 h to purify the COF nanosheets. During dialysis, some COF nanosheets agglomerated into dispersed precipitates, which could be easily removed from the colloidal suspension by subsequent filtration through nonwoven paper. Finally, a homogenous COF nanosheet dispersion with a concentration of 0.0506 wt% or 0.5047 g/L and a pH value of 3.27 was obtained. 2.2.3. Fabrication of the COF interlayer and modification The as-prepared DABA-TFP COF nanosheets were coated onto nylon substrates via the vacuum filtration technique. Prior to use, the nylon membrane was thoroughly washed with DI water or methanol to remove any impurities and then placed into the vacuum filtration apparatus.

Specific volumes of the COF nanosheet dispersion (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mL) were accurately measured and diluted to a total volume of 50 mL with either DI water or methanol. The diluted dispersion was subjected to ultrasonication for 1 min to ensure uniform distribution. Next, the diluted dispersion was carefully poured onto the nylon mem­brane. The system was allowed to stand for 1 min without vacuum application, allowing the dispersion to settle evenly. A vacuum was subsequently applied to remove the solvent, and filtration was continued until the membrane surface appeared completely dry. Therefore, the COF–H+-nylon membrane was obtained. For further modification, the COF–H+-nylon membrane was immersed overnight in 50 mL of NaOH solution with a pH of 10 or saturated NaHCO3 solution with a pH of 8‒9 to obtain the modified COF–Na+-nylon membrane. The DABA-TFP COF nanosheets were also coated onto YSZ hollow fibres via a vacuum-coating technique. Initially, a 4 cm YSZ hollow fibre was fixed onto a sample holder with epoxy resin. After the hollow fibres were washed with DI water and methanol, they were connected to a vacuum pump and immersed in a COF nanosheet dispersion made by diluting 1 mL of the original COF nanosheet solution in 49 mL of methanol.

Fabrication of the PA active layer The PA layer was synthesized using an IP method followed by an additional thermal post-treatment. The substrate or interlayer/substrate (nylon, COF–H+-nylon, or COF–Na+-nylon) were placed in a vacuum filtration apparatus and then thoroughly washed by filtrating DI water through the membranes for at least 5 min under vacuum. Subsequently, 10 mL of aqueous MPD solution at designated concentrations (2 wt%, 3 wt%, 4 wt%, 5 wt%, and 6 wt%) was poured onto the membrane, which was immersed for 3 min. After immersion, the membrane was placed vertically under ventilation conditions and air-dried for 5 min. Subse­quently, 10 mL of 0.15 wt% TMC in n-hexane was applied to the membrane surface. The IP reaction was allowed to proceed for 1 min. The reaction was then terminated by thoroughly washing the membrane with n-hexane. A thermal curing step was performed by placing the membrane vertically in an oven at 60 ◦C for 10 min to obtain a PA–COF–Na+-nylon TFC membrane.

Pure solvent permeance Various organic solvents were tested on the fabricated composite membranes. A dead-end filtration system was employed for the permeation test with the membrane fixed in a flat-sheet membrane cell. An O-ring with an 18 mm diameter was positioned on top of the mem­brane, providing an active membrane area of 0.00025 m2. The permeate was collected in a sealed container to prevent solvent loss due to evaporation, and the volume of the collected permeate was recorded. During the test, 100 mL of solvent was added to the membrane cell, and a pressure of 20 bar was applied. The system was first operated for 15 min to allow the permeance to stabilize, and the final reported value was calculated as the average over the subsequent 60 min. To ensure reproducibility, at least three identical membrane samples were tested. The permeance value was calculated using the following equation: P(L m-2h-1bar-1)=VA * Δt * ΔpEq 4 where V represents the volume of the collected permeate (L), A repre­sents the active membrane area (m2), Δt represents the duration of measurement (hour), and Δp represents the transmembrane pressure (bar).

Characterization of the DABA-TFP COF nanosheets As depicted in Fig. 2A, the synthesis of COF nanosheets involves the polycondensation of the sulfonated monomer DABA with TFP. During the reaction, the aldehyde groups of TFP react with the amine groups of DABA, forming β-ketoenamine linkages [18]. The DABA-TFP COF nanosheets were synthesized via a reported single solution-phase syn­thesis in which two monomers were polymerized at room temperature for 2 days in DMSO without any other coagents [18]. The reaction occurred very quickly, with the transparent solution turning red immediately. As the reaction progressed, the color of the solution gradually changed from light red to dark red. The obtained solution was homogenous without any precipitation and exhibited an obvious Tyn­dall effect, indicating a good suspension of the COF nanosheets in DMSO (Fig. 2B). The sheet-like morphology of the nanosheets is further confirmed by the images shown in Fig. S2. The successful formation of the DABA-TFP COF was confirmed by FT-IR characterization. As shown in Fig. 2C, the consumption of monomers could be confirmed by the disappearance of C––O groups (1640 cm-1) of TFP and N–H groups (3300–3400 cm-1) of DABA in the spectra of DABA-TFP [20]. The transverse dimensions of the COF nanosheets ranged from 40 to 100 nm, whereas the thicknesses of the COF nanosheets ranged from 2.5 to 4 nm (Fig. 2E). Therefore, the width of the nanosheets is orders of magnitude greater, resulting in a high aspect ratio ranging from 10.3 to 36.4. The narrow distribution of thickness is likely attributed to the balance between attractive forces (such as π‒π stacking and van der Waals interactions) and electrostatic repulsion among the COF layers, which stabilizes the structure within the colloidal dispersion [18]. To quantitatively assess the flake size of the COF nanosheets in different dispersion media, dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was performed on the COF nanosheet dispersions in both water and meth­anol. As depicted in Fig. 2F, the hydrodynamic diameter of the COF nanosheets dispersed in water predominantly ranged between 100 and 800 nm, with a prominent peak at approximately 350 nm. In contrast, the COF nanosheets dispersed in methanol exhibited relatively larger sizes characterized by a significant peak ranging from 6 to 16 μm. Additionally, the polydispersity index (PDI) increased from 31.09% in water to 42.73% in methanol, indicating a greater tendency for the COF nanosheets in methanol to aggregate, forming larger assemblies.

Membrane fabrication and characterization 3.2.1. Fabrication of the COF interlayer The first step in constructing the TFC membrane involves the fabri­cation of the COF interlayer, where DABA-TFP COF nanosheets selfassemble onto the nylon substrate via vacuum filtration. When 1 mL of the initial COF nanosheet dispersion was diluted in water or methanol and then vacuum-assembled onto the nylon surface (Fig. 3A), the water dispersion resulted in poor surface coverage, leaving much of the nylon surface exposed (Fig. 3B), whereas the methanol dispersion resulted in a continuous COF layer fully covering the nylon surface (Fig. 3C). This occurred because the COF nanosheets dispersed in water exhibited relatively small flake sizes, which could pass through the nylon substrate into the permeate during the vacuum filtration process, leading to poor surface coverage.

As shown in Fig. S4, the pristine nylon substrate initially exhibited a highly uneven surface with an average roughness (Ra) of 74.1 nm. Upon the introduction of a COF interlayer, the surface roughness decreased markedly as the COF filled uneven areas, progressively forming a smoother and more continuous surface. The Ra value decreased dramatically to 32.6 nm at a COF loading of 0.25 mL. With increasing COF loading, the surface roughness further decreased to 22.8 nm at a COF loading of 0.5 mL, followed by 13.8 nm at a COF loading of 1 mL. In addition, the porometer data presented in Fig. S5 indicate a reduction in pore size and an increase in porosity with increasing COF loading. As the COF loading increased from 0 to 0.25 mL, the mean pore size gradually decreased from 286 nm to 142 nm and further decreased to 19.8 nm at a loading of 0.5 mL. In contrast, the porosity slightly increased from 2.3% for the pristine nylon substrate to 3.5% at 0.25 mL, reaching 8.1% at 0.5 mL COF loading. These results suggest that increasing the COF effectively reduces the pore size while increasing the overall porosity.

This failure can be attributed primarily to the large surface pore size of the nylon substrate, which is 286 nm (Fig. S6–a), leading to two key challenges: 1) the substantial pore size enables the IP reaction to occur within the pores, leading to the intrusion of the formed PA into the substrate rather than the formation of a uniform and continuous layer on the surface; and 2) the large pore size results in insufficient capillary action, which limits the holdup of the MPD solution necessary for the IP reaction [24]. In contrast to the suboptimal IP reaction conditions provided by the surface of nylon, a COF–H+ interlayer with a reduced surface pore size and increased surface smoothness offers a more favourable platform for the IP process. Consequently, a continuous PA layer formed on the COF–H+ interlayer, as evidenced in Fig. 4B, demonstrating significantly improved surface coverage.

content: . Consequently, a continuous PA layer formed on the COF–H+ interlayer, as evidenced in Fig. 4B, demonstrating significantly improved surface coverage. However, despite this improvement, the resulting PA layer still exhibited a significant number of defects. These observed defects were attributed primarily to the presence of sulfonated groups within the DABA-TFP COF structure. While these sulfonated groups are essential for enabling the single solution-phase synthesis of COF nanosheets, they adversely affect the formation of the PA layer. Specifically, the H+ ions dissociated from the sulfonated groups can react with the –NH2 active sites in MPD, leading to the consumption of the diamine monomers. This interaction hinders the IP reaction, ulti­mately compromising the quality of the PA layer by introducing defects. This is also evidenced by XPS analysis (Fig. S7), which reveals that the PA layer fabricated on the COF–H+ interlayer exhibited a higher content of protonated amine species (NH3+, 4.98%) compared with those fabri­cated on the COF–Na+ interlayers (2.67% or 1.45%). This indicates that, in the absence of alkaline treatment, a larger fraction of MPD is pro­tonated, thereby reducing its nucleophilicity and potentially hindering its reaction with TMC during IP.

To mitigate the adverse effects of the dissociated H+ ions of the COF interlayer on the formation of the PA layer, the COF–H+ interlayer was treated with either NaOH or NaHCO3, facilitating the substitution of H+ with Na+ ions, thereby forming the COF–Na+ interlayer. The ion ex­change process, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 5A, can be explained by an acid‒base neutralization reaction, wherein the dissociated H+ ions react with HCO3-or OH-present in the solution, allowing the negatively charged sulfonated groups to form stable ionic bonds with Na+. The successful formation of the COF–Na+ interlayer was confirmed through XPS elemental analysis, as shown in Fig. 5B and Table S3–1. A characteristic peak of Na 1s appeared in both NaHCO3and NaOHtreated COFs, indicating that the Na content increased from 0.83% to 1.40% or 3.36% after alkaline treatment with NaOH or NaHCO3, respectively. Therefore, effective replacement of H+ with Na+ can be simply achieved by immersing the COF–H+ membranes in either NaOH or NaHCO3 solutions while the morphology of the interlayers did not change (Fig. S8).

To further examine the chemical stability of the resulting COF–Na+ interlayers in polar protic solvents, XPS analysis was conducted before and after immersion in methanol overnight, revealing that the Na 1s signal remained clearly detectable with only a minor change in atomic percentage (Fig. S9), indicating that the Na+ ions were largely retained within the sulfonated COF framework under the tested conditions. The effect of COF treatment on PA formation is substantial. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the PA layers formed on the COF–Na+ (0.5 mL) in­terlayers treated with NaOH or NaHCO3 exhibited a continuous and defect-free surface with nanoscale crumpled features. The continuous surface is expected to facilitate precise solute separation, whereas the crumpled texture increases the effective surface area, thereby enhancing the solvent permeance of the resulting PA–COF–Na+-nylon membrane. The cross-sectional images (Fig. 4E and F) show that there were no obvious boundaries observed between the COF interlayer and the PA layer, demonstrating excellent compatibility between those two mate­rials due to their similar compositions of light elements [25]. Therefore, the thickness of the PA layer can be calculated only by subtracting the interlayer thickness from the total thickness above the nylon substrate.

content: In summary, as demonstrated in Fig. 6D, directly fabricating a continuous PA layer on a microporous substrate via IP synthesis is difficult. While adding a COF–H+ interlayer with a smaller pore size and smoother surface is helpful in forming a continuous PA layer, its strong acidity introduces defects, leading to poor performance. However, after simple alkaline treatment with NaHCO3 or NaOH, a defect-free PA layer with excellent retention can be obtained. This method is applicable for both organic and inorganic substrates, demonstrating that the COF–Na+ interlayer can effectively eliminate differences between substrates and promote the effective growth of the PA rejection layer on microporous substrates with high performance. Based on the previously discussed findings, we further examined the influence of the COF–Na+ interlayer thickness on the performance of the PA–COF–Na+-nylon membranes. A series of PA–COF–Na+-nylon mem­branes with varying COF–Na+ loadings (0.25 mL, 0.5 mL, 1 mL, and 2 mL) were fabricated, with the PA layer synthesized under identical IP conditions. The rejection performance results, as illustrated in Fig. 7A, indicate that all the membranes exhibited high rejection (over 90%) for all 4 neutral dyes despite differences in interlayer thickness. Even for COF–Na+ loadings of 0.25 mL, which displayed some surface defects (Fig. 3E), the resulting PA membrane achieved good rejection perfor­mance.

The gradual increase in rejection observed for smaller solutes after DMF activation is attributed to solvent-induced conditioning of the PA layer [27]. Immediately after activation, DMF penetrates the poly­mer network and induces a transient swollen state with a relatively open dense layer. As DMF is gradually displaced by methanol, polymer chains undergo slow rearrangement toward a more compact and stable configuration, resulting in enhanced rejection, particularly for low-molecular-weight solutes. Compared with the performance before DMF activation, the rejec­tion of VB12 and BrB slightly increased, whereas the rejection of the two relatively smaller dye molecules slightly decreased (Fig. 8C, Table S5), making the membrane more selective. The slight improvement in the rejection of the two relatively larger molecules can be attributed to swelling-induced compression, which may help eliminate pre-existing defects in the PA layer [28]. In contrast, the slightly reduced rejection of the two relatively smaller molecules is attributed to the formation of a looser PA network induced by DMF activation [29]. When dye rejection was evaluated in DMF (Fig. 8D, Table S5), the rejection of VB12 and BrB remained high, exceeding 90%, although slightly lower than those measured in methanol.

Meanwhile, the rejec­tion of C6 and DMP in DMF was lower than that in methanol, with values of 62.9% and 48.8%, respectively. This behaviour is likewise associated with the DMF-induced swelling effect [30], which has a more pro­nounced impact on the transport of smaller solute molecules. To further evaluate the stability of the membrane in practical phar­maceutical separation, we tested the permeance of the membrane across pressures ranging from 1 to 30 bar. As depicted in Fig. 8E, the methanol flux increased linearly with pressure, demonstrating the excellent resistance of the membrane to compaction under pressure. Moreover, we evaluated the permeance stability of the PA–COF–Na+-nylon mem­brane toward various common organic solvents, including highly aggressive solvents such as THF and DMF. The solvent permeances after DMF activation are presented in Fig. 8F and Table S6. The as-prepared membrane exhibited stable performance in all the tested solvents, which suggests its potential for application in various solvent systems.

The solvent permeances of the TFC membranes decreased in the following order: methanol (9.95 ± 0.96 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) > DMF (5.26 ± 0.27 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) > acetonitrile (4.29 ± 0.47 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) >acetone (2.81 ± 0.91 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) > ethanol (2.70 ± 0.46 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) > THF (0.46 ± 0.02 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) > 1-propanol (0.16 ±0.03 L m-2 h-1 bar-1). This exceptional high-pressure stability, com­bined with its outstanding resistance to organic solvents, suggests that the membrane can be effectively utilized under a wide range of oper­ating conditions. Finally, this membrane was performed in pharmaceutical separation. Artemisinin, a drug well known for its antimalarial properties, is typi­cally extracted from organic solvents soaked with Artemisia annua. In this study, we investigated the artemisinin enrichment performance of the PA–COF–Na+-nylon membrane in ethanol, which is a commonly used solvent for artemisinin extraction because of its high extraction yield and safety [31]. After DMF activation, this membrane demon­strated excellent performance in artemisinin enrichment, with a high rejection exceeding 98%, as observed from a significant reduction in the concentration from 100 ppm in the feed to approximately 2 ppm in the permeate (Fig. 9, Fig. S11).

content: 98%, as observed from a significant reduction in the concentration from 100 ppm in the feed to approximately 2 ppm in the permeate (Fig. 9, Fig. S11). With high rejection, the membrane effec­tively retained artemisinin while allowing ethanol to permeate, ensuring a concentrated solution with minimal loss of the target compound. The test was performed for 3 days, and the rejection performance of the membrane was highly stable throughout the test. The retentate after 3 days was highly concentrated to 338 ppm (Fig. 9, Fig. S11), demon­strating the reliability and effectiveness of the artemisinin enrichment of the PA–COF–Na+-nylon membrane. Notably, the current study focuses on the final-stage enrichment of artemisinin and solvent recovery using a model system. Future work will explore membrane cascade designs and cross-flow operation with real extracts to assess long-term stability and fouling behaviour. Compared with other OSN membranes, the PA TFC membrane developed in this work showed superior performance, surpassing the upper bound, in terms of methanol permeance and dye rejection of the molecules between 236 and 327 g/mol (Fig. S12, Table S7). This study will broaden the application of OSN membranes in the pharmaceutical industry. 4. content: in the pharmaceutical industry. 4. Conclusion In conclusion, this study developed a sulfonated COF interlayer for the polymerization of a PA membrane. A state-of-the-art single-phase synthesis was employed to synthesize a DABA-TFP COF nanosheet dispersion with a high yield in a straightforward and effective manner. Then, the sulfonated 2D COF nanosheets were vacuum coated onto a microporous nylon 66 substrate under the aggregation effect of meth­anol. The obtained COF–H+ interlayer provided a smooth and uniform surface with a reduced pore size, serving as an optimal platform for the IP reaction and successfully preventing the PA intrusion that typically occurred when nylon was used directly as the substrate. After the post treatment of COF–H+ with either NaHCO3 or NaOH, the as-formed COF–Na+ interlayers mitigated the negative effects of H+ on the IP process, enabling the formation of a continuous and defect-free PA layer. This COF–Na+ interlayer was also successfully applied to inorganic microporous supports, achieving a similar effective rejection perfor­mance of the PA layer on YSZ hollow fibres as that observed on nylon. These findings demonstrate that the developed method is not only simple but also versatile, with high reproducibility and significant po­tential to work effectively with various types of membrane substrates.

The PA–COF–Na+-nylon membrane demonstrated a high methanol permeance of 9.95 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and excellent rejection performance above 94% for both charged and uncharged molecules. The resulting membrane was further utilized in the enrichment of artemisinin, an active pharmaceutical component typically extracted from natural plants by immersion in organic solvents. The optimized PA–COF–Na+- nylon TFC membrane achieved approximately 98% rejection of arte­misinin from ethanol, effectively enriching artemisinin while simulta­neously recycling ethanol. This approach facilitates easier extraction of artemisinin from concentrated solutions, significantly reducing energy consumption and minimizing solvent waste.
